text: We designed and performed creating a bicuspid valve for the RVOT from the patient's own right atrial appendage in 2014.
The most frequently asked question is that the RAAs come in different shapes and sizes.
Which ones are suitable for valve creation and which ones are not?

the best RAAs for valve creation are quadrangular and tall and the worst are short arch shape and wide but the good thing is that this is a very elastic tissue especially when you remove the muscles so it accommodates itself with the shape that you want look at this one in the first look it seems impossible to be used as a valve
It is too short, 4-5 mm, but you will see how we make it suitable with some maneuvers and we use it with very good result.
In our experience, almost 90% of appendages can be used for valve creation.
Bivalve cannulation is performed and cardiopulmonary bypass is started.
To have the ideal function as a bicuspid valve, the width of the base of the appendage should be 1.5 times the RA height, which can be seen here in this case.
However, very rarely the use of RA valve is abandoned due to RA anatomy.
The final decision to create the valve is made only after cardioplegic arrest and complete evaluation of the anatomy.
Right ventriculotomy and pulmonary arteriotomy are performed between traction sutures.
The valve and the annulus are evaluated and only when it is not possible to preserve the valve, the annulus is opened.
Pulmonary arteriotomy is extended into the bifurcation and RVOT myectomy is performed as usual.
The VSD is then closed.
There is a moderate-sized subaortic VSD in this case with severe overriding of aorta.
The VSD is enlarged to prevent subaortic stenosis and is then closed by a piece of bovine patch.
Now it is time to prepare the RAA.
Two 6-0 traction sutures at the corners are pulled toward the surgeon and the fine attachments between the RAA and the AV groove are released by fine scissors.
This is the course of right coronary artery.
And usually this dissection is far and safe.
This is a very important step and you can see that you increase the height at least a few millimeters.
Now this is the other patient with very short four millimeter appendage.
Now you see that how much increase in the height has occurred only by this maneuver.
from 4-5 mm to 17 mm.
The SA node and the borders of the appendage are marked.
A fine cross clamp is put in the proper position far enough from the SA node and the RCA.
Horizontal mattress suturing is done underneath the clamp.
Two 6-0 traction sutures are put at the basal corners and the RA is cut over the clamp.
The atrial stem closure is finished by another row of continuous lock suturing.
The forest state sutures are fixed.
The upper border is lifted by the assistant and the internal trabeculations are excised.
Large muscle bands are removed, but small trabeculations are left in place.
Care is taken not to make holes.
However, small accidental holes may be repaired by 7-0 sutures.
The distal end is then cut and opened to make a tube from the RAA.
Colleagues also ask about the size of the RVOT.
The native surface of the RVOT should be approximately half the circumference of the proper Heger sizer
for that body surface area of the patient.

In normal TF patients, this almost always matches, but in some cases with dilated RVOT and PA, like absent pulmonary valve syndrome patients, you have to excise a part of the wall, while in very small PAs, like in some pulmonary atresia patients, you have to add a type of patch to the border.
The valve is placed in the RV OT with the proximal end in the annulus position.
The proximal end is sutured in a straight line at the native annulus.
If the main PA is short, a new annulus is made a few millimeters inside the RV to prevent the obstruction of PA branches by the distal part of the valve.
First, the right corner of the annulus is fixed and then the suturing with 6 oprolin starts from the left and finishes at the right.
The posterior annular line is now finished.
The commissural line suturing starts from distal to proximal, while both the PA valve and the RAA valve are stretched to have the longest possible commissural height.
This is very important as it guarantees a better long-term function of the valve.
Now the whole area should be covered by a patch for which we use bovine patch.
The size of the patch is very important and the length and widths of the patch are measured precisely.
We use silk threads for this measurement.
The widths in the proximal and distal ends of the valve should be equal to their native counterpart.
Suturing of the patch starts at the most distal point in the bifurcation area and continues on both sides until it reaches the annulus.
At this point, the patch is folded back and the anterior border of the proximal end of the RAA valve is sutured to the fold.
Care is taken not to make purse string effect in the suture line.
The RA valve can now be tested by the blood flow return from the pulmonary artery or by injecting water when the distal PA is clamped.
In almost all of our patients, we have observed that the valve is completely competent at the end of the operation and the echocardiography afterwards has confirmed the same results.
In our experience, the leaflet made from the lateral wall of the appendage
has a slightly larger contribution and movement.
Either it is better for long-term results to put this leaflet anteriorly or posteriorly, we have not reached a conclusion yet.
The patch extension for proximal RVOT is sutured and the operation is finished.
